findings.detail: Cross-unit contradiction guts the par argument. The btree checked-lib route's entire node representation is 'keys: seq<K, inline B>, vals: seq<V, inline B>, children: seq<handle, inline B+1>' embedded in a pool slot — i.e. it load-bears on seq's inline knob being (a) kept and (b) embeddable as a struct field. But the primitives-audit unit in the SAME re-cut trims exactly that member ('seq's inline (SmallVec) knob → defer the knob behind a bench trigger') and independently asserts 'B-tree nodes need uninit affine slot arrays, so no user BTreeMap at par exists.' Both verdicts cannot land. If the trim lands, each node degrades to three heap-allocated seqs: +3 dependent loads and 3 allocator hits per node touched, leaf (K,V) contiguity destroyed. Mechanical cost: lookup goes from h slab derefs to ~4h dependent loads (~1.5-2x latency, blowing the 1.10x band), and range scan loses the pointer-bump loop entirely (2-4x off the 2ns/element target). Ordered map over SQLite-shaped workloads is at least medium-high weight, and the gap is far beyond 25%. Additionally, even if inline seq survives, 'embeddable in a user struct inside a pool slot, with insert-at/remove-at/drain-range' is an unpinned property of the sealed kernel (elsewhere described as a three-word-header heap object). Either primitives-audit's seq-inline trim is revoked (revising its claimed spec savings) or btree reverts to KEEP-SEALED/defer; the adjudicated set is internally inconsistent as written.

The remove/reinsert rebalancing protocol is mechanically underspecified in a way that adds cost or breaks scans. pool.insert after remove mints a NEW generational handle, invalidating every inbound handle — and inbound handles are not only the parent's child slot (which the route rewrites) but the sibling leaf chain: the predecessor leaf's next_leaf now points at a dead generation, so the leaf-chained range cursor hits a generation-check failure (or must treat it as end-of-range) after any split/merge. Fixing it requires doubly-linked leaves plus rewriting both neighbors on every structural change (more handle rewrites and an extra slab touch per split), or the optional checked disjoint two-slot &uniq pool borrow — which the spec-savings section itself calls optional and possibly absent. The 'few cycles per insert amortized' rebalancing estimate is computed for the incomplete protocol; the complete one is still probably inside the 1.15x insert band, but the perf argument as written has not actually costed a correct b-tree.

The partition-loop elision claim rests on a fact-system capability that does not exist yet: type-range facts (u8 value < 256 usable as an index bound). The unit concedes this in its own risks and quietly budgets 'one spec clarification' — i.e., the CUT verdict is contingent on shipping a new fact-language feature, which is exactly the fact-channel class requiring hostile review before it can be leaned on. Without it, the block partition — the most bounds-dense inner loop in the whole catalog (one indexed load + one indexed store per element at ~1ns/element budget) — keeps a data-dependent bounds check per element on both the offset-generation and swap passes; a plausible 15-30% hit on the loop that sets ipnsort's 55-80M/s figure, which can exceed the 25% medium-weight threshold. The D9a retained-bounds precedent (percent-encoding, utf8parse) came from far less bounds-dense kernels and does not transfer. Separately, the radix scatter's index (running prefix-sum position) is semantically bounded but not provable by any dominated-bounds pattern described, so 'bounds elide' does not cover that loop at all; it is memory-bound so likely <10%, but it should be carved out of the elision claim and preregistered. The falsifier does fail closed, but the verdict should be recorded as conditional on the fact-system extension, not on the current floor.

Two attacks. (1) Perf: the fixed-offset half is genuinely at par (constant-offset domination is the easy case), but the data-dependent half — SQLite cell-pointer walks, TCP options, TLV — assumes the requires system can carry a runtime 'off + w <= len' fact across an access site and, worse, an element-wise fact over a materialized validated-offset array. That is asserted from a system measured only on dominated constant/loop-index bounds (base64). If the fact language is scalar-and-constant only, every access in a page-walk loop keeps a bounds branch; the loop body is only ~2 loads + compare per cell, so even a well-predicted branch is a 5-15% tax on a corpus-adjacent workload (SQLite is one of the four frozen projects), uncomfortably close to the FATAL line and not covered by any shipped measurement. (2) Ergonomics: the cut moves offset arithmetic from a machine-checked schema into hand-chained consts written by modest-capability AI writers from the spec alone. A wrong offset type-checks, passes the safety checker, and reads the wrong field silently — the exact structural bug class the schema mechanism existed to make unrepresentable. 'Card discipline plus round-trip tests' is unenforceable by the checker; for a language whose premise is that entire bug classes are unrepresentable, this cut re-admits one on the write-heavy format-parsing path.

The Copy-ring card as specified contains mechanical errors that a modest AI writer will faithfully reproduce. (1) The as_slices recipe '[head&m .. cap] and [0 .. tail&m]' is only correct when the live region wraps; when head&m < tail&m the correct answer is the single slice [head&m .. tail&m], and the card's formula returns garbage regions that type-check and pass the safety checker. (2) The floor's `buffer` is defined as fixed-length; a growable ring therefore cannot actually be 'a fixed buffer of pow2 capacity' — it must be built over seq (or a runtime-length boxed slice), which changes what fact must be established (pow2 fact about seq.len, flowing through the sealed container's API) and is a strictly harder elision case than a static-length buffer. (3) The single load-bearing perf assumption — that the fact system proves (i & (cap-1)) < cap from a pow2 prologue fact, a nonlinear/bitwise inference — is untested; if it fails, every access in a ~1ns-per-op loop keeps a bounds branch and the Copy route misses par outright. The CUT-DEFER conclusion is probably right, but the card needs the unwrapped case fixed, the backing-store story corrected, and the mask-fact proof demonstrated before it can be called at-par.

The seq-inline defer is a one-way door bet against the project's own flagship workload. The unit's own risks admit both halves: xlc (the self-hosted compiler, the most visible program the language will ever run) is exactly the SmallVec-hungry AST/token shape the inline knob exists for, and seq's three-word-header ABI can be frozen into extern boundaries or serialized layouts before the 1.10x bench trigger ever fires, making later re-admission a breaking layout change. Arena-backed seq recovers allocation amortization but not the branch-free no-indirection access of a true inline buffer for len<=4 vectors; SmallVec-class wins in compiler workloads are routinely 5-15% end-to-end, which fails the 1.10x band on a HIGH-visibility program. Combined with the btree unit's hard dependency on the same knob (see FATAL), the correct adjudication is likely 'keep seq-inline, take the trim only on table's inline=N' — the tiny-map linear-scan replacement is the defensible half of this trim, the seq half is not.

The perf argument is unusually honest (the O(n)-republish update ceiling and the uncovered high-churn regime are stated, not hidden). Two residual nits. (1) The boxed-affine-V read path's 'one more pointer chase, ~5-15ns' risk should be compared against the actual papaya mechanism: papaya also heap-allocates entries and derefs on read, so the card is at parity there, not behind — the card text should pin that comparison so the 1.3x latency band isn't mis-scored against an idealized in-place reference. (2) The M7 falsification bands are built on an assumed ~50-100ns/entry sealed-table clone cost with no whitefoot measurement behind it; if table iteration+insert is 2-3x that, the 10^4 updates/s band fails for reasons unrelated to the trim's logic. Measure the clone constant before treating band (b) as a meaningful falsifier.

Mechanically the soundest cut in the set — the commit path is fdatasync-bound by 2-3 orders of magnitude and the card issues the identical syscall sequence, so sealing buys nothing. Residuals: (1) the verdict is conditional on three file leaf rows (fdatasync, rename, directory-handle fsync) plus an alignment-honoring O_DIRECT open that are asserted, not verified present; ratifying the cut before pinning the row set inverts the dependency (verify the rows first, then ratify). (2) No floor row exists for querying the device logical block size, so O_DIRECT on 4Kn/512e edge devices is unhandled. (3) Payload memcpy is performed under the leader mutex; at 4KB x 16 committers this serializes ~3us of copy per batch — irrelevant against NVMe fdatasync but worth restructuring (copy outside, reserve under lock) in the shipped lib so the card doesn't teach the lazy shape.

The cas-cell cut argument is genuinely strong — the residual raw-CAS protocols are unbuildable on this floor anyway, so the member cannot complete any user program — and steal demotion changes visibility, not code, since users cannot spawn the threads a hand scheduler needs. Two watch items: (1) the relaxed counter as a misused done-flag is a silent-misuse channel; the 'no publication' rule must be normative spec text, not card prose, because a modest writer who reads a counter as a ready signal gets a checked, memory-safe, wrong program; (2) by the unit's own D16 standard, condvar's keep is the weakest in the set (queue endpoints may cover all v1 blocking-wait patterns) and should carry the same preregistered re-examination the cuts got, rather than riding through on classical-pair sentiment.

Two card-level mechanism errors. (1) The tiny-map card claims 'linear-scan small-map over seq<(K,V)> with machine-core SIMD key compare' — SIMD compare+movemask cannot run over keys interleaved with values in a tuple seq (strided keys defeat the vector load); the card needs parallel keys/vals seqs, or should honestly claim a scalar early-exit scan (which is fine for n<=8 and probably still passes the 1.05x band, but the stated mechanism is wrong as written). (2) The unit's FIFO story contradicts the ring unit: it claims 'no user route to VecDeque's contiguous ring (uninit affine slots again)' — false for Copy T, where the fully-initialized masked ring needs no uninit storage — and then blesses a two-stack card that is strictly dominated by the ring unit's masked-ring card for Copy elements. Two competing taught spellings for the same job violates the one-spelling doctrine and steers modest writers into the worse card; reconcile to one FIFO card (masked ring for Copy, pool-handle for affine, two-stack only as the affine amortized fallback).

The LZ overlap-copy contingency is handled correctly (fall back to one sealed wbuf member, copy_within_extend, rather than keeping the whole schema mechanism), but note the fallback member itself is fact-adjacent trusted surface (it writes into the extent based on caller-supplied offset/len) and must be budgeted for hostile review in the spec-savings accounting, which currently books it at zero. Also the 0.97x/CI-excluding-0.95x Gate 2 band is tighter than any other unit's; fine as ambition, but it should pin x86-64 and AArch64 explicitly per the unit's own ISA-variance risk or the band will fail spuriously on targets where C struct overlay is equally degraded.

overall: The re-cut is mostly mechanically honest — io-wal, sync-trim, and shared-rcu are well-argued cuts whose par claims survive attack (fdatasync dominance, unbuildable residual CAS protocols, and an honestly-conceded update ceiling respectively) — but the set contains one fatal internal contradiction and a cluster of verdicts that lean on compiler capabilities that do not exist yet. The FATAL: btree's CUT-TO-CHECKED-LIB is built entirely on seq<T,inline> embedded as struct fields, while primitives-audit trims that exact member and independently declares a user btree impossible; both verdicts cannot land, and whichever way it resolves, either primitives-audit's spec savings shrink or btree reverts toward KEEP-SEALED. The recurring pattern behind the MAJORs is fact-system overdraft: three units (ring's pow2-mask proof, bytescan's u8 type-range facts, layout-cursor's runtime and element-wise offset facts) discharge their hottest bounds checks with fact-language features that go beyond the one measured capability (dominated constant/loop bounds, base64 1.71x), and each such feature is itself a fact channel requiring hostile review before it can carry a par claim. Second-order: two taught cards contain mechanism errors as written (ring's as_slices formula wrong for the unwrapped case and its fixed-length-buffer backing story; tiny-map's SIMD-over-interleaved-tuples), and layout-cursor knowingly re-admits a silent wrong-offset bug class to modest AI writers that the schema mechanism made unrepresentable. Recommended disposition: resolve the btree/seq-inline contradiction before ratifying anything (most likely fix: keep seq-inline, take only table's inline=N trim); mark bytescan, layout-cursor, and ring as conditional on named, hostilely-reviewed fact-system extensions with their preregistered checks run before the verdicts bind; ratify io-wal (after verifying the file leaf rows exist), sync-trim, and shared-rcu as-is with the noted card-text fixes.

findings.unit: primitives floor — typed uninitialized affine slots (cross-cutting justification for seq, table, pool, arena, and the ring/btree/stable-sort deferrals)
findings.detail: The single load-bearing justification for nearly every sealed container keep is the floor's ban on typed uninitialized affine slot storage and take/replace out of user containers. That ban is a project design choice, not an immovable fact, yet the adjudication never prices the alternative: one checked language primitive instead of four-plus sealed kernels. The checker already proves two harder-looking disciplines — wbuf's initialized-byte-extent judgment and pool's generation-witnessed occupancy — so the claim that a typed analog is unverifiable is asserted nowhere and examined nowhere. Graded honestly: a typed PREFIX-EXTENT buffer (initialized prefix + len over affine T, the exact discipline seq already enforces internally) would let users build seq and the arena emplacement path as zero-trust checked source at par (same instruction shapes: bump/len increment, memcpy grow, move-out at the extent boundary), collapsing roughly 25-30 sealed op rows and 1.5-2.5k trusted LOC into one language rule cluster; it would also un-defer the inline-affine ring and the stable-merge scratch that two verdicts list as permanent gaps. Arbitrary-hole disciplines (table's control-byte-keyed slots, pool's freelist) are genuinely harder and may legitimately stay sealed — but D16 places the burden on the seal, and a catalog that seals four kernels because the floor withholds one expressible judgment fails that burden until the primitive is explicitly adjudicated and rejected with a named checker limitation. This must be answered before the catalog shape is ratified; every container verdict downstream inherits it.

The btree CUT-TO-CHECKED-LIB verdict is built entirely on seq<T,inline> with small fixed inline capacity being embeddable as a struct field inside a pool slot ('the floor's critical gap ... is exactly what seq<T,inline> already provides sealed'), with insert-at/remove-at/drain-range. The primitives-audit verdict in the same re-cut TRIMS the seq inline knob ('defer the knob behind a bench trigger', deleting the dual-representation rows and promotion rules). Both cannot land: if the trim ships, btree's node representation collapses back to handle-indirection and its own risk #1 fires — the par argument for affine K/V fails and btree reverts toward KEEP-SEALED; if btree's route ships, the seq trim's claimed spec savings are partly fictitious because a fixed-inline-capacity embeddable seq mode must exist (and note btree needs a NON-promoting fixed-capacity variant, which is arguably a third representation, not the trimmed SmallVec knob). The adjudication is internally inconsistent and one verdict must be rewritten: either keep a fixed-capacity embeddable inline-seq mode with its rows explicitly charged to the btree route's cost line, or re-adjudicate btree as deferred/sealed. The tiny-vector bench trigger in primitives-audit does not resolve this because btree's need is structural (embeddability), not a performance band.

findings.unit: sync publish/snapshot cell × shared<T> — two sealed spellings of read-mostly publish
findings.detail: The kept catalog carries both a publish/snapshot cell in the sync vocabulary (justified by 'publish-new-immutable-state / read-mostly config swap', reference arc-swap) and shared<T> epoch-pinned publish/snapshot (kept in shared-rcu, reference arc-swap/RCU). These are the same capability class with two sealed spellings, violating the one-spelling doctrine and D16 simultaneously. The cell is only coherent if it is restricted to Copy-word payloads (a load-acquire scalar cell with no reclamation question); the moment it publishes a boxed/heap value it must answer who reclaims the old value — refcount (the shared-line write that kills the M7 scaling argument), leak, or epoch, i.e., it degenerates into shared<T>. The adjudication never delimits the cell's payload domain. Required: either merge the cell into shared<T> (one object, one rule) or pin the cell to Copy scalars with an explicit 'no heap publication — use shared<T>' rule line, and re-verify that sync-trim's cas-cell coverage argument (which routes config-swap scenarios to the cell) still holds under the restriction.

findings.unit: io family — unenumerated keep; evring carried on zero corpus evidence
findings.detail: The io family is kept wholesale as 'syscall surface' without a member enumeration, making it the only sealed object whose op-row count is unauditable — and it is plausibly the largest. Within it, evring (io_uring-class submission/completion, linked ops, registered buffers — realistically 10-20 op rows and 2-3k trusted LOC of the most security-sensitive kind, kernel-shared-memory lifecycle) has no consumer anywhere in the frozen fourteen-operation corpus: SQLite's ops need pwrite/fdatasync/rename/dir-fsync, and the io-wal cut uses evring only as an 'optional fast path' whose falsifiable check runs entirely on synchronous file rows. Under D16 lean-beats-complete, evring is deferrable at zero cost — sealed syscall surfaces are purely additive later — and keeping it now reads as tokio/liburing-era ecosystem mirroring rather than scenario evidence. Same hygiene demand for any net members riding inside 'family'. Required before ratification: enumerate the io family row-by-row, attach a named v1 scenario to each leaf, shrink to file plus the wal-required durability rows, and move evring to the deferred lane with a preregistered admission trigger (a v1 scenario whose par target demonstrably needs high-QD async submission).

findings.unit: catalog accounting — no honest normative-surface count anywhere in the re-cut
findings.detail: The adjudication claims savings per unit but never totals the residual catalog, which D16 review requires. Honest estimate of the post-cut sealed surface: ~10 sealed objects (seq, table, pool, arena, threads+par, sync x5 members, conc_queue x2 endpoints, shared<T>, io family, extern-C records), roughly 90-110 sealed op rows, and order 15-30k trusted LOC (scheduler ~3-5k, SwissTable ~2-3k, io ~3-5k+, conc_queue ~2-3k, sync ~2k, seq/pool/arena ~2-3k). The cuts removed roughly 45-60 rows and 8-12k trusted LOC (ring, btree, rcu_table, wal, layout schema, sort core, cas-cell, steal, two inline knobs) — real progress, but the residual sealed catalog still rivals or exceeds the 90-rule kernel spec itself, i.e., the trusted library is on track to be a second spec larger than the language. Worse, member-level audits were applied only to sync, conc_queue, and the two inline knobs; the two largest trusted-LOC pockets (threads+par scheduler surface and the io family) never received the row-by-row scenario-evidence treatment that sync-trim got. Required: publish the count (objects / rows / trusted LOC, pre vs post), and run the sync-trim-style member audit on threads+par and io before the catalog freezes.

findings.unit: FIFO story — two competing taught spellings with conflicting preregistered bands (ring card vs primitives-audit two-stack card)
findings.detail: The ring unit ships a Copy masked-ring composition card (pow2 buffer, monotone counters, fact-elided masked bounds) with a preregistered BFS-frontier check banded at [0.90x, 1.10x] of VecDeque, while primitives-audit independently nominates a two-stack FIFO card for the same v1 FIFO job with a BFS check banded at 1.15x and a conceded 2x move constant. That is two blessed writings for one job — a direct one-spelling-doctrine violation for exactly the audience (modest AI writers) the doctrine protects — plus two different preregistered pass bands for the same scenario class, which invites post-hoc band shopping when one fails. The two-stack card is strictly dominated for Copy payloads (it exists only as the affine fallback, where the pool-handle ring is also documented). Required: collapse to one primary FIFO card (the Copy masked ring), demote two-stack to the affine-fallback subsection only, and reconcile the BFS falsification bands into a single preregistered number before any benchmark runs.

findings.unit: sync condvar × io-wal — load-bearing dependency recorded as speculative surface
findings.detail: sync-trim keeps condvar on 'classical-pair grounds' and flags it as the weakest keep that 'a later trim pass should re-examine', while io-wal's entire group-commit route (leader-follower over mutex+condvar) makes condvar load-bearing for a ratified cut. If a later trim pass takes sync-trim's risk note at face value and cuts condvar, the wal checked-lib silently loses its concurrency substrate. Cheap fix: record io-wal's leader-follower pattern as condvar's named scenario evidence in the sync member list, converting the keep from speculative to evidenced and closing the cross-unit trap.

findings.unit: machine core intrinsic list — no admission rule; the next padding vector
findings.detail: The bytescan verdict already anticipates adding intrinsic rows on demand ('the fix is adding the intrinsic row to machine core, not sealing the kernel' for clmul/pdep), and crc32c is assumed present by both bytescan and io-wal. Each named intrinsic is a normative spec row plus a codegen guarantee, and 'add a row whenever a kernel wants one' is exactly how std::arch-mirroring accretion happens without any single addition looking wrong. Required: a one-line admission rule for machine-core rows (named consumer kernel in the taught library or a corpus operation, plus a portable fallback semantics statement), and an explicit current-list freeze so additions go through the same D16 gate as sealed members rather than riding in as benchmark fixes.

findings.unit: arena capability — spelled across three normative clusters
findings.detail: The floor names generative arena brands (language primitive), lexical regions/arenas (language primitive), and the arena kernel (sealed component) as three separately listed surfaces for one capability story. If brands are the checker mechanism, regions the scoping rule, and the kernel the allocator, the layering may be sound — but the catalog should present it as one spec cluster with one rule set, not three line items, or reviewers cannot tell whether the arena story is one mechanism or three accreted ones. Consolidate the presentation and charge the whole cluster as one unit in the surface count.

The shared-rcu trim keeps shared<T> at ~5 rows 'optionally update-with', while its own card text routes all multi-writer mutation through the external mutex and merely asks that this be stated on the card. Under D16, an 'optional' row with no scenario that the mutex+publish pair fails is convenience padding; carrying it also weakens the crisp one-rule story ('readers write zero shared lines') by adding a writer-side semantics row. Cut update-with from v1; it is purely additive later if a scenario shows the mutex route missing a band.

spsc survives on mechanism grounds (store+load vs mpmc's CAS, rtrb/crossbeam ~10-20ns reference), but its only scenario evidence is the pre-nominated 'pipeline / bounded producer-consumer' entries in sync-trim's necessity sweep — no frozen-corpus operation touches it (Crossbeam's corpus op is the steal deque, now internal to par). That is acceptable only because the sweep is preregistered; the keep should be explicitly conditioned on the sweep showing mpmc misses the 1.10x band on the pipeline kernels. If mpmc lands in-band there, spsc should be deferred rather than kept — a sealed endpoint added later is purely additive, and the two-endpoint page reads leaner still as one.

findings.unit: extern-C records — no v1 corpus consumer named
findings.detail: extern-C records are definitionally sealed (ABI layout is a codegen guarantee) and small, but the re-cut never names their v1 consumer: the fourteen frozen operations are reimplementations, not FFI linkages, and the io family's syscall ABI needs are internal to sealed kernels, not user-facing rows. Either record the concrete v1 scenario that requires user-visible extern-C records (e.g., linking a pinned C reference for differential testing, or a declared FFI target), or trim the user-facing rows to the minimum the sealed io family itself forces and defer the rest. As written it reads as 'a systems language obviously needs FFI' — habit, which is exactly what the attack lens exists to catch.

overall: The re-cut's eight verdicts are individually well-argued and the cuts are real — roughly 45-60 op rows and 8-12k trusted LOC removed — but the keeps do not yet clear D16's burden, for two structural reasons and one bookkeeping one. Structurally: (1) almost every container seal is justified by the floor's ban on typed uninitialized affine slots, a self-imposed design choice the adjudication never prices; the checker already ships the byte-extent (wbuf) and occupancy-witness (pool generations) disciplines, so at minimum a typed prefix-extent buffer primitive — which would unseal seq and arena emplacement and un-defer the inline ring and stable-sort scratch — must be explicitly adjudicated and rejected with a named checker limitation before the catalog shape is ratified. (2) The re-cut is internally inconsistent: the btree cut requires the embeddable seq<T,inline> mode that primitives-audit trims, and the FIFO job has two competing taught spellings with conflicting preregistered bands — both must be reconciled before any preregistered benchmark runs, or the falsification apparatus itself is compromised. Bookkeeping: no honest total was ever published; the residual sealed catalog (~10 objects, ~90-110 op rows, order 15-30k trusted LOC) rivals the 90-rule kernel spec, and its two largest pockets — the threads+par scheduler and the still-unenumerated io family (including evring, which no frozen-corpus operation needs) — never received the member-level audit that sync got. Recommended disposition: hold ratification; require (a) the typed-slot primitive adjudication, (b) resolution of the seq-inline/btree and FIFO contradictions, (c) a row-by-row io and scheduler audit with per-row scenario evidence and deferral of evring, (d) merge or delimit the publish/snapshot cell against shared<T>, and (e) publish the pre/post surface count as a standing table in the plan. None of this reverses the cuts already made — it finishes the same lean pass on the parts of the catalog it skipped.
